sections.heading: Introduction
sections.title: Introduction to internet safety regulation framework
sections.summary: This section establishes the overarching purpose of the Online Safety Act 2023 to regulate internet services for individual safety in the UK. It mandates providers of regulated services to identify, mitigate, and manage risks of harm from illegal content and activities harmful to children. The Act also grants new regulatory powers to OFCOM, the UK communications regulator.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets the foundation for cross-border data transfer considerations by establishing the regulatory framework that requires services to be safe by design and provide higher protections for children. It creates obligations that may impact data flows involving children and illegal content.

requirements.text: Providers must identify, mitigate and manage risks of harm from illegal content and activities harmful to children
Providers must identify, mitigate and manage risks of harm from illegal content and activities harmful to children
Regulator OFCOM is granted new functions and powers

sections.source_quote: This Act provides for a new regulatory framework which has the general purpose of making the use of internet services regulated by this Act safer for individuals in the United Kingdom.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and child safety mandates

sections.heading: Providers of user-to-user services: duties of care
sections.title: Duties of care for user-to-user services
sections.summary: This section outlines specific duties that providers of user-to-user services must comply with, including risk assessments for illegal content, content reporting, complaints procedures, freedom of expression and privacy protections, and record-keeping. It also specifies additional duties for services likely to be accessed by children and Category 1 services, which include enhanced protections for democratic content and journalistic activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes detailed obligations for user-to-user services that may affect data transfers involving children and require specific safeguards for illegal content, impacting cross-border data flows and child data protection.

sections.heading: Illegal content risk assessment duties
sections.title: Illegal content risk assessment duties
sections.summary: This section requires providers of user-to-user services to conduct suitable and sufficient illegal content risk assessments at specified intervals. It mandates that these assessments be kept up to date and that providers take appropriate steps to review them when changes are made to the service. The assessment must evaluate the user base, risks of encountering illegal content, risks of facilitating priority offenses, and potential harm to individuals.

sections.heading: Safety duties about illegal content
sections.title: Safety duties about illegal content
sections.summary: This section requires providers to design and operate services to prevent users from encountering priority illegal content, mitigate risks of the service being used for illegal activities, and protect individuals from harm. It mandates specific actions such as taking down illegal content swiftly, implementing content moderation, and ensuring terms of service clearly address illegal content protections. Category 1 services have additional duties related to democratic and journalistic content.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific safety duties for illegal content that may impact data transfers involving children and require providers to implement technical and procedural safeguards for illegal content, affecting cross-border data flows.

sections.heading: Assessment duties: user empowerment
sections.title: User empowerment assessments for adult users
sections.summary: Category 1 services must conduct suitable and sufficient assessments to evaluate adult user empowerment risks. These assessments must consider user base, relevant content incidence, likelihood of exposure to harmful content, group-specific vulnerabilities, service functionalities, and governance measures. The assessments must be updated regularly and aligned with the service's proposed changes. The assessments are required to be conducted at specific intervals as defined in Schedule 3.

sections.heading: User empowerment duties
sections.title: User empowerment features for adult users
sections.summary: Category 1 services must implement features that allow adult users to control content exposure, including filtering non-verified users. These features must be accessible, easy to use, and include mechanisms for users to retain or change settings. Services must also provide clear terms of service explaining these features and summarize recent assessment findings. The features must reduce exposure to harmful content or alert users to specific harmful content types.

sections.heading: Duties to protect content of democratic importance
sections.title: Protection of democratic content
sections.summary: Category 1 services must implement systems to protect content of democratic importance, including news publisher content. These systems must ensure free expression is considered when taking actions against content or users. The duties require consistent application across political opinions and clear terms of service explaining the protection mechanisms. The content must be intended to contribute to democratic political debate in the UK.

The provider must also reverse actions if the news publisher requests it within a reasonable period.

sections.heading: CHAPTER 4
sections.title: Procedure for issuing codes of practice
sections.summary: This section outlines the procedure for OFCOM to submit draft codes of practice to the Secretary of State for parliamentary approval. If Parliament rejects the draft within 40 days, OFCOM must prepare a revised draft. Otherwise, the code becomes effective after 21 days. The process includes consultation with relevant stakeholders and adherence to specific timelines for amendments and reviews.

sections.heading: "Illegal content" etc
sections.title: Definition of illegal content and relevant offences
sections.summary: This section defines 'illegal content' as content that constitutes a relevant offence. 'Relevant offence' is defined as either a priority offence or an offence specified in subsection (5). An offence is within subsection (5) if it is not a priority offence, the victim is an individual, and the offence is created by the Act or other specified legislation. The definitions also specify priority offences, terrorism content, CSEA content, and intimate image content. The section clarifies that content's jurisdiction is not limited to the UK and includes automated tools.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines key terms for content classification, critical for determining what constitutes illegal content under cross-border services. Specifies that content jurisdiction is not UK-only, affecting how data is classified globally.

sections.heading: CHAPTER 3
sections.title: Duty not to remove user content without terms of service compliance
sections.summary: A Category 1 service provider must not remove regulated user-generated content, restrict user access, or suspend/ban users without following their terms of service. Exceptions include actions taken to comply with legal duties protecting individuals from illegal content or children from harmful content, or to avoid criminal/civil liability. The duty applies to each Category 1 service operated in the UK and only covers UK users.

Complaints must meet criteria for importance or large user impact. Eligible entities must represent user interests and are subject to regulatory consultation before complaint procedures are established.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines user-based complaint mechanisms that could impact data transfer assessments when user data is involved in service compliance.

requirements.text: An eligible entity may make a complaint to OFCOM that any feature of regulated services or conduct presents significant harm to users or affects freedom of expression
A complaint relating to a single service or provider is only admissible if OFCOM considers it of particular importance or relates to a large number of users
An entity is eligible if it meets criteria specified in regulations, including representing user interests
Regulations must specify that the entity represents user interests or a particular group

sections.source_quote: An eligible entity may make a complaint to OFCOM that any feature of one or more regulated services, or any conduct of one or more providers of such services, or any combination of such features and such conduct is, appears to be, or presents a material risk of causing significant harm to users of the services or members of the public.
sections.relevance_reason: User representation for complaints

sections.title: Consultation and parliamentary procedure for strategic priorities
sections.summary: This section sets out requirements for the Secretary of State to consult OFCOM and other persons before designating strategic priorities. The consultation must include a 40-day response period for OFCOM, and the statement must be laid before Parliament before designation. The 40-day period excludes periods when Parliament is dissolved or prorogued.

sections.title: Appeals against OFCOM decisions on content notices
sections.summary: This section outlines procedures for appealing OFCOM decisions regarding notices on terrorism content, confirmation decisions, and penalty notices. Appeals can be brought by persons with sufficient interest in the decision. Appeals must follow judicial review principles and may result in dismissal or quashing the decision. If quashed, the Upper Tribunal must remit the decision to OFCOM for reconsideration with appropriate directions.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides procedural safeguards for challenging OFCOM decisions; relevant for DTIAs when assessing appeals against content-related notices.

requirements.text: An appeal to the Upper Tribunal against OFCOM's decision to give a notice under section 121(1) may be brought by any person with a sufficient interest in the decision
An appeal against a decision may be brought only with the permission of the Upper Tribunal if the appellant is not the person given the notice or decision
The Upper Tribunal must decide appeals by applying the same principles as the High Court on judicial review
The Upper Tribunal may dismiss an appeal or quash the decision being challenged

sections.title: Threatening communications offence definition and elements
sections.summary: This section defines the threatening communications offence requiring the sender to convey a threat of death or serious harm, with intent or recklessness that the recipient would fear the threat would be carried out. 'Serious harm' includes grievous bodily harm, rape, assault by penetration, or serious financial loss. For financial threats, a defence exists if the threat reinforces a reasonable demand and the sender reasonably believed it was a proper means to achieve that demand.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for threatening communications that could impact data transfers involving threats of harm, requiring assessment of threat severity, intent, and potential financial implications.

requirements.text: The person sends a message conveying a threat of death or serious harm
At sending time, the person intended an individual to fear the threat would be carried out or was reckless about that fear
Serious harm includes grievous bodily harm, rape, assault by penetration, or serious financial loss
For financial threats, a defence exists if the threat reinforces a reasonable demand and the sender reasonably believed it was a proper means to achieve that demand

sections.heading: PART 10
sections.title: False communications offence definition and elements
sections.summary: This section defines the false communications offence requiring the sender to send a message containing false information, intend to cause non-trivial psychological or physical harm to a likely audience, and have no reasonable excuse for sending it. The likely audience includes individuals who would encounter the message or share it online. The offence has specific penalties including a maximum term of 51 weeks or a fine up to level 5 on the standard scale, with proceedings limited to 6 months from evidence discovery and a 3-year window from offence commission.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for false communications offences that could impact data transfers involving harmful content, requiring assessment of intended harm, audience reach, and reasonable excuses for sending messages across borders.

sections.title: Definition of director for member-managed bodies
sections.summary: This section defines 'director' for a body corporate whose affairs are managed by its members as a member of that body. It specifies that a partner of a Scottish partnership commits an offence under section 184 if the offence was committed with their consent or connivance or is attributable to their neglect. It also defines 'partner' for Scottish partnerships to include anyone purporting to act as a partner.

requirements.text: In paragraph (a) 'director', in relation to a body corporate whose affairs are managed by its members, means a member of the body corporate.

If an offence under section 184 is committed by a Scottish partnership and it is proved that the offence has been committed with the consent or connivance of a partner of the partnership, the partner commits the offence.

If an offence under section 184 is committed by a Scottish partnership and it is proved that the offence is attributable to any neglect on the part of a partner of the partnership, the partner commits the offence.
'Partner', in relation to a Scottish partnership, includes any person who was purporting to act as a partner.

sections.heading: PART 11
sections.title: Providers' judgements about content status
sections.summary: This section outlines how providers of Part 3 services must make judgements about content types, including illegal content and fraudulent advertisements. Providers must base judgements on reasonably available information, consider factors like provider size and moderation methods, and follow specific tests for reasonable grounds to infer content types. OFCOM may assess compliance with these judgements.

requirements.text: Judgements about content types must be made on the basis of all relevant information reasonably available to a provider.
Factors relevant to information availability include provider size and capacity, and whether judgements are made by human moderators, automated systems, or a combination.

Reasonable grounds for inferring content types exist if a provider has reasonable grounds to infer all elements of an offence are present and does not have reasonable grounds to infer a defence may be successfully relied upon.
For bot-generated content, the tests for reasonable grounds apply to the conduct or mental state of a person who may control the bot.
OFCOM may take into account whether providers' judgements follow the approaches set out in this section when assessing compliance.

sections.source_quote: This section sets out the approach to be taken where a system or process operated or used by a provider of a Part 3 service for the purpose of compliance with relevant requirements involves a judgement by a provider about whether content is content of a particular kind.

sections.source_quote: 219 (part 2) Power to amend Act: alternative dispute resolution (217) Power to impose duty about alternative dispute resolution procedure (1) The Secretary of State may by regulations amend this Act for or in connection with the imposition on providers of Category 1 services of an ADR duty. (2) An 'ADR duty'— (a) is a duty requiring providers of Category 1 services to arrange for and engage in an alternative dispute resolution procedure in specified circumstances for the resolution of disputes about their handling of relevant complaints, and (b) may include a duty requiring such providers to meet the costs incurred by any other person in using a dispute resolution procedure which is so arranged. (3) Complaints are 'relevant' for the purposes of subsection (2)(a) if they— (a) relate to a Category 1 service, (b) are of a specified kind, and (c) are made by persons of a specified kind. (4) Regulations under this section may not be made before the publication of a statement by the Secretary of State responding to OFCOM's report under section 160 (report about reporting and complaints procedures in use by providers of Part 3 services: see subsection (10) of that section). (5) Before making regulations under this section the Secretary of State must consult— (a) OFCOM, (b) (c) (6) If the power conferred by subsection (1) is exercised, the first regulations made under the power must— (a) require the use of a dispute resolution procedure which is impartial, and (b) prohibit the use of a dispute resolution procedure which restricts or excludes the availability of civil proceedings. (7) Provision made by regulations under this section may have the effect that the duties set out in any or all of sections 21, 22 and 23 which apply in relation to duties imposed by other provisions of Chapter 2 of Part 3 are also to apply in relation to the ADR duty, and accordingly the regulations may amend— (a) section 21(6), (b) the definition of 'safety measures and policies' in section 22(8), or (c) the definition of 'relevant duties' in section 23(11). (8) The provisions of this Act that may be amended by the regulations in connection with the imposition of the ADR duty include, but are not limited to, the following provisions (in addition to those mentioned in subsection (7))— (a) section 7(5), (b) section 104(13)(a), and (c) section 131(2). (9) If the power conferred by subsection (1) is exercised, the first regulations made under the power must require OFCOM to— (a) produce and publish guidance for providers of Category 1 services to assist them in complying with the ADR duty, and (b) consult the Secretary of State, the Information Commissioner and such other persons as OFCOM consider appropriate before producing the guidance. (10) Section 204(1) applies for the purposes of the references to Category 1 services in this section. (11) In this section 'specified' means specified in regulations under this section. 217A Power to impose duties about pornographic content (1) The Secretary of State may by regulations amend this Act for or in connection with the imposition, on providers of regulated services or regulated services of a particular kind, of duties relating to— (a) verifying the age of individuals appearing in pornographic content published or displayed on those regulated services ('age verification duties'); (b) verifying whether individuals appearing in pornographic content published or displayed on those regulated services consent to the content being published or displayed ('consent verification duties'). (2) Age verification duties and consent verification duties may include duties relating to the systems and processes used to operate regulated services, or regulated services of a particular kind. (3) Consent verification duties may include duties relating to cases where consent previously given is withdrawn. (4) Regulations under this section may make provision requiring OFCOM to issue guidance to providers of regulated services, or regulated services of a particular kind, to assist them to comply with age verification duties and consent verification duties. (5) The provisions of this Act that may be amended by the regulations in connection with the imposition of age verification duties and consent verification duties include, but are not limited to, section 131(2).

sections.title: Definition of proactive technology and related terms
sections.summary: This section defines key terms including 'proactive technology', 'content identification technology', 'user profiling technology', and 'behaviour identification technology'. It specifies the conditions under which certain technologies are excluded from being classified as proactive technology. The definitions are contextual to regulated services and user data.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical technical terms relevant to content monitoring and user profiling, which may impact how data is processed and monitored across services. These definitions are foundational for understanding data handling requirements in regulated environments.

requirements.text: Content identification technology means technology, such as algorithms, keyword matching, image matching or image classification, which analyses content to assess whether it is content of a particular kind (for example, illegal content).

User profiling technology means technology which analyses (any or all of) relevant content, user data, or metadata relating to relevant content or user data, for the purposes of building a profile of a user to assess characteristics such as age.

Behaviour identification technology means technology which analyses (any or all of) relevant content, user data, or metadata relating to relevant content or user data, to assess a user's online behaviour or patterns of online behaviour (for example, to assess whether a user may be involved in, or be the victim of, illegal activity).

Relevant content means in relation to a user-to-user service, content that is user-generated content in relation to the service; in relation to a search service, the content of websites and databases capable of being searched by the search engine; in relation to an internet service within section 80(2), content that is provider pornographic content in relation to the service.

User data means data provided by users, including personal data (for example, data provided when a user sets up an account), and data created, compiled or obtained by providers of regulated services and relating to users (for example, data relating to when or where users access a service or how they use it).

sections.source_quote: 231 'Proactive technology' (1) In this Act 'proactive technology' means— (a) content identification technology, (b) user profiling technology, or (c) behaviour identification technology, but this is subject to subsections (3) and (7).

sections.title: Factors determining public/private content communication in user-to-user services
sections.summary: This section defines factors OFCOM must consider to determine whether content communicated via user-to-user services is 'publicly' or 'privately' accessible. It specifies three key factors: the number of UK individuals who can access the content, restrictions on access (e.g., requiring approval), and the ease of forwarding content to other users or services. These factors apply specifically to content relevant for terrorism notices, proactive technology requirements, and code of practice recommendations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines how content accessibility determines whether user-to-user services are considered 'public' or 'private' for regulatory purposes, impacting cross-border data transfer assessments by clarifying when content is broadly accessible versus restricted.

sections.heading: Limited functionality services
sections.title: Exemptions for limited functionality user-to-user and search services
sections.summary: This section outlines specific exemptions for user-to-user services and search services that meet certain criteria, such as limited functionality, internal business use, or provision by public authorities. These exemptions allow services to avoid certain obligations under the Online Safety Act 2023 if they operate within defined parameters like restricted user interactions or specific service contexts.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides exemptions for services that handle user-generated content with limited functionality, relevant for data transfer assessments involving international services.

requirements.text: A user-to-user service is exempt if the functionalities of the service are limited, such that users are able to communicate by means of the service only in the following ways—
posting comments or reviews relating to provider content
sharing such comments or reviews on a different internet service
expressing a view on such comments or reviews, or on provider content, by means of—
applying a "like" or "dislike" button or other button of that nature
applying an emoji or symbol of any kind
engaging in yes/no voting
rating or scoring the content (or the comments or reviews) in any way (including giving star or numerical ratings)
producing or displaying identifying content in connection with any of the activities described in paragraphs (a) to (c)
provider content means content published on a service by the provider of the service or by a person acting on behalf of the provider
content that is user-generated content in relation to a service is not to be regarded as provider content in relation to that service
A user-to-user service is exempt if the only user-generated content enabled by the service is content of the following kinds—
content mentioned in paragraph 1, 2 or 3 and related identifying content
content arising in connection with any of the activities described in paragraph 4(1)
But a user-to-user service described in any of paragraphs 1 to 5 is not exempt if—
regulated provider pornographic content is published or displayed on the service
the service has links with the United Kingdom within the meaning of section 80(4)

sections.summary: Part 3 services must complete their first illegal content risk assessment within three months of becoming a Part 3 service or transitioning to one, provided illegal content risk assessment guidance is available but CAA guidance has not yet been published. sections.summary: OFCOM must ensure codes of practice for Part 3 services align with online safety objectives, including proportionality, technical feasibility, and user protection. The objectives require services to protect UK users from harm through effective risk management systems.

sections.heading: SCHEDULE 10
sections.title: OFCOM's recovery of initial costs
sections.summary: This section outlines the process for OFCOM to recover initial costs from telecommunications providers. It specifies the calculation of additional fees based on the aggregate of Wireless Telegraphy Act receipts retained by OFCOM. Providers must pay these fees over specified charging years, with a mechanism for recovery in subsequent years after the initial charging period ends.

sections.heading: End of the recovery process
sections.title: End of recovery process for additional fees
sections.summary: This section defines when the process for recovering additional fees from regulated services ends. It states that the process concludes if OFCOM's statement records that the recoverable amount is nil or all recoverable amounts are likely to be paid. Alternatively, the Secretary of State may end the process by determining that OFCOM will not charge additional fees. The earliest determination can be made after OFCOM's first statement. The determination must be made promptly and published appropriately. The determination does not affect OFCOM's ability to recover fees or act under section 141 for liabilities existing before the determination.

sections.heading: Power of entry and inspection without a warrant
sections.title: Entry and inspection without warrant: notice and conditions
sections.summary: OFCOM may enter and inspect premises of regulated service providers without a warrant if they believe the premises are used for regulated services and have given seven days' notice. Entry must occur at a reasonable hour. Authorized personnel must produce identification and state the purpose if requested. They may inspect documents, equipment, and require information relevant to regulated services. Copies of documents may be taken during inspections. The powers are limited to what is necessary for OFCOM's functions under this Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for OFCOM's warrantless entry and inspection of premises, requiring advance notice and specific procedural steps that impact data transfer assessments by defining when and how inspections can occur without warrants.

sections.title: Powers exercisable by warrant
sections.summary: An authorized person executing a warrant may enter premises, search, inspect documents and equipment, require information, produce copies, and seize documents or equipment.
